segments.s325-g33: This clause begins with the temporal adverb 'کنون' (now) followed by the perfective verb 'یافتم' (I found), so the speaker states a completed action in the past relative to the narration. 'هرچه جستم' is a relative/indefinite phrase 'whatever I sought' where the verb 'جستم' is first-person past of 'جستن' (to seek), and 'ز کام' is a prepositional phrase 'from (my) desire' using the poetic preposition 'ز'. Word order (Adv + Verb + Object phrase) is typical of classical/Ferdowsian Persian inversions for emphasis.
segments.s325-g34: This short clause uses the impersonal/obligatory auxiliary 'بباید' (it must / it is necessary) followed by 'پسیچید' and then the nominal phrase 'کآمد خرام'. The verb forms are archaic/poetic; 'پسیچید' here functions as a verbal predicate meaning roughly 'is followed' or 'comes after' (passive/impersonal sense in context), and 'کآمد' is a demonstrative/third-person past ('that which came' / 'it came') with the participial adjective 'خرام' describing its gait ('proud, strutting'). The whole unit links to the previous clause as a consequence: having obtained one's desire, it is fitting/necessary that what came now proceed in proud gait.
segments.s325-g35: The temporal phrase 'سحرگه' ('at daybreak') opens the clause. 'مرا' appears split in the word list into 'م' + 'را' but functions together as the first-person object 'me' (i.e. 'my' / 'to me'); here it marks the experiencer of the following state. 'چشم بغنود' is a poetic past construction meaning 'the eye fell asleep' or 'the eyes grew heavy/closed', with 'دوش' ('last night') providing the temporal reference — in classical word order the elements are flexible, so the fact that 'دوش' appears at the end is a stylistic inversion meaning 'last night/my eyes fell asleep (and thus were heavy) at daybreak'.
segments.s325-g36: This is a straightforward prepositional clause: 'ز یزدان' ('from God') + the past tense verb 'بیامد' ('came'), followed by the adjective 'خجسته' ('auspicious/blessed') modifying the noun 'سروش' (a Soroush, i.e. a divine messenger or revelation). Word order is Subject/Agent omitted — 'a blessed Soroush came from God' — and the construction is plain past narration.
segments.s325-g37: 'که' introduces a subordinate content clause ('that' / 'which'). 'برساز' is an archaic/poetic verb form here meaning roughly 'bring about / proclaim' (literally 'make/bring'), and it governs the following nominal clause 'کآمد گه رفتنت' — 'کآمد' ('it came') combined with 'گه رفتنت' ('the time/occasion of your departure'). The sequence conveys reported speech or the content of the messenger's announcement: it declared that the time of your leaving had arrived.
segments.s325-g38: This elliptical clause continues the announced news: 'سرآمد' here is used as 'the end/conclusion (has come)', followed by two coordinated nouns 'نژندی' (disgrace, humiliation) and 'ناخفتنت' (literally 'your not-sleeping' = 'your sleeplessness'). The conjunction 'و' simply links the two nouns. Grammatically, the verb 'سرآمد' functions as a predicate whose implicit subject is the state described — i.e. 'an end came to disgrace and to your sleeplessness.'
segments.s325-g39: This clause begins with 'کنون' (now) and the subject phrase 'بارگاه من' (my court/palace). The verb 'آمد' (came) is past; 'به سر' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'upon' or 'to the head of' — in idiomatic classical Persian 'آمد به سرِ X' means 'X befell' or 'X came upon (someone)'. So the structure is 'Now my court came under / was visited by' whatever follows (here, grief).
segments.s325-g40: This is a noun series headed by 'غم' (grief/sorrow) followed by coordinated nouns 'لشکر' (army), 'تاج' (crown), 'تخت' (throne) and 'کمر' (girdle/strength). The list describes the domains or objects affected: grammatically these nouns are governed by the prepositional idea in the previous segment ('came upon my court regarding/over the army, crown, throne, and girdle').
segments.s325-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” بشنید is the simple past (3rd sg.) “he heard.” In this poetic order the verb phrase (چو بشنید) comes before the subject خسرو (Khosrow); ز + دستان is a poetic prepositional phrase “from/of the dāstān(s)” (here meaning source of the speech), and سخن is the noun “speech/words.”
segments.s325-g2: یکی دانشی is an indefinite noun phrase “a certain piece/one of knowledge” (دانشی with the -ی suffix = ‘a kind of/one’). پاسخ is the direct object “answer/reply,” and افگند is the past tense verb “he cast/put forth.” بن here functions adverbially in the sense ‘down/thereon’ (poetic), so the whole clause means he threw forth/let fall a learned reply.
segments.s325-g3: بدو is the prepositional pronoun “to him/unto him” placed before the verb (common in older/poetic Persian). گفت is the past-tense verb “said” (here used as ‘he said to him’). کای is the vocative particle “O,” and پیر پاکیزه مغز is the vocative noun phrase “elder (pīr) of pure mind” (پاکیزه as adjective modifying پیر, مغز specifying mind/intellect).
segments.s325-g4: This is the content of the speech. همه رای و گفتارهای تو is a coordinated noun phrase “all the counsels/opinions and your utterances” (تو governs گفتارهای as a possessive). نغز is a predicate adjective placed after the coordinated nouns meaning “excellent/pithy/witty.” The adjective applies to both رای and گفتارهای.
segments.s325-g5: ز گاه ... تا این زمان is a temporal range: ز گاه منوچهر “from the time/age of Manuchehr” تا این زمان “until this time/now.” Note the poetic use ز for از and the noun order (source then limit).
segments.s325-g6: نه‌ای is a negative existential/form of ‘to be’ used here to mean essentially ‘there has been nothing’ (negated existence). جز introduces the exception ‘except.’ بی‌آزار و نیکی گمان is a coordinated noun phrase “harmlessness and good (or kind) intention/belief” (گمان here = thought/intention), the thing excepted by جز.
segments.s325-g7: همان is a demonstrative “that/the same” used as an identifying particle; نامور is an epithet “renowned/famed.” رستم پیلتن is the proper name with a place-epithet (Rostam of Pīltan). In Persian poetic word order epithets and place-names often follow the demonstrative and adjective.
segments.s325-g8: This is a string of nominal epithets in apposition: ستون کیان is a genitive construction meaning “pillar/support of the Kians” (ستون = pillar/support; کیان = the Kian kings/royal house). ناز ش انجمن reads as نازِشِ انجمن (ناز + ش = ‘his/their pride’) انجمن ‘assembly’ so together: “the pride of the assembly.” The two phrases are parallel epithets praising Rostam.
segments.s325-g17: وگر is a conditional 'if'. The phrase 'نام و رنج تو' is a coordinated object ('your name and your suffering') placed before the verb. گیرم is 1st-person present/subjunctive (literary) meaning 'I take/keep' (here conditional: 'if I keep'), and the prepositional phrase به یاد ('into memory / in remembrance') attaches to the verb to indicate holding something in memory.
segments.s325-g18: بماند is a present/subjunctive verb meaning 'will remain / shall remain' used after the conditional clause. 'سخن تازه' is the subject phrase 'the new/fresh saying/poem', and تا صد نژاد indicates duration/extent ('for/up to a hundred lineages/generations') with تا here meaning 'as far as / up to'.
segments.s325-g19: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/about' or 'on account of' introducing the phrase 'ز گفتار چرب' ('from/for the flattering speech'). ار is an archaic conditional 'if'; پژوهش کنم is a present-subjunctive 1st-person 'I examine / I investigate' (کنم is the auxiliary/verb 'I do'). The word order places the cause/topic before the conditional verb.
segments.s325-g20: ترا is written as a combination of the 2nd-person object suffix ت (here listed separately) plus the object marker را; together they mark 'you' as the direct object. این ستایش نکوهش are two coordinated nouns ('this praise [and] reproach') governed by the single verb کنم ('I will do/make'); idiomatically it means 'I will both praise you and reproach you.'
segments.s325-g21: دگر here means 'the rest / other' (i.e. 'as for the remainder'), and هرچه پرسیدی is 'whatever you asked' with پرسیدی in the past addressing 'you' (2nd person). از کار من means 'about my work/affairs' where از functions as 'concerning'. The whole segment sets up a topic for explanation.
segments.s325-g22: ز here functions causally ('because of'). نادادن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'not giving / withholding' (a negative of دادن); it governs the following objects بار و آزار من ('burden and harm to me'). The phrase thus gives the reason: 'because I was not given (or was spared) burden and affliction.' Note brief ambiguity whether it means 'I did not give' or 'I was not given'; context favors 'I was not given / I was spared.'
segments.s325-g23: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'to/unto God' (addressing the divine). یکی آرزو is the noun phrase 'one wish' or 'a single desire', and داشتم is past tense 'I had' (first-person), so the whole line states a single desire addressed to God in the past.
segments.s325-g24: جهان را uses را as the definite object marker ('the world' as object). همه خوار is a predicative phrase 'entirely despised / wholly contemptible' and بگذاشتم is past 1st-person 'I left / I set / I made' used here causatively: 'I made/treated the whole world as despised' or 'I set the world aside as worthless.' Word order (object before verb) and the use of را are typical literary constructions.
segments.s325-g25: کنون 'now' opens the line (temporal adverb). پنج هفتست is a formulaic way to indicate a span — literally 'five seven is' — here conveying 'five or seven' / 'several' (a vague time span). تا introduces the temporal clause 'since/until' linking that time span to the speaker's action. من is the first-person subject 'I'. به + پای literally 'to/at the foot (of)' — a locative expression meaning 'at the feet (of someone)' and marks the place of the speaker's supplication.
segments.s325-g26: همی is the classical/progressive particle marking habitual or continuous action ('still/continually'). خواهم is first-person present/future of خواستن and here expresses the speaker's ongoing wish/action 'I seek' or 'I keep asking'. از is the preposition 'from'. داور in this devotional context functions as 'the Judge' (a divine/judicial figure). رهنمای is a noun 'guide' and is the object of the seeking (guidance).
segments.s325-g27: که here is a subordinating conjunction introducing the wished-for/result clause ('that' / 'so that' / 'who'). بخشد is the third-person subjunctive/optative form of بخشیدن ('may he forgive' / 'let him forgive') expressing hope or petition. گذشته modifies گناه and means 'past' (so 'past sin'). مرا (split here as مر + را) marks the possessor/object 'of me' or 'my' — the sin belongs to the speaker; را is the definite/object marker attached to that pronoun.
segments.s325-g28: درخشان is an adjective 'bright, shining' used predicatively. کند is the third-person subjunctive/optative of کردن used here as 'may he make' (parallel to بخشد — a wished result). تیره modifies گاه to form the compound 'dark time / dark hour(s)'. مرا + را again mark that 'the dark time' pertains to the speaker (object/possessive). The clause as a whole expresses the hoped-for outcome: 'may he make my dark time bright.'
segments.s325-g29: برد here functions as a verbal root meaning 'took/removed (away)' (classical past sense 'carried off / removed'). مر + مرا are emphatic/oblique forms of the first-person object 'me' (double/clitic forms occur in classical verse for emphasis), and ز این سپنجی سرای is a prepositional phrase 'from this sponge-like house/dwelling' (سپنجی 'sponge-like/porous', سرای 'house/dwelling'). The clause describes being removed/withdrawn from the mortal/spongy dwelling.
segments.s325-g30: بود is the past tense of 'to be' (here 'he was'). در همه = 'in/with respect to all' (a distributive/totalizing phrase). نیکوی is an adjective 'good/excellent' qualifying the following noun. رهنمای is the noun 'guide' — the clause says he was an excellent guide in every respect.
segments.s325-g31: نماند is the negative of ماندن used impersonally: 'it does not remain / there is no longer (possibility)' — idiomatically 'I cannot / there is no way'. کز is a fused form of 'که' + 'از' often meaning 'that/from which' or simply introducing the content clause. این is the demonstrative 'this'. راستی is a noun meaning 'truth/uprightness/honesty' (here 'this uprightness' or 'this course of right action'). بگذرم is the first-person present/subjunctive of گذشتن 'to pass/leave' but in context 'to abandon' or 'to depart from' (i.e. 'that I should abandon this uprightness').
segments.s325-g32: چو is a comparative/conditional particle here meaning 'as / like' (or 'if/when' in conditional sense); شاهان is the plural 'kings'; پیشین means 'former/ancient/the kings of old'. یپیچد is a classical third-person singular verb 'twists/turns' (here describing the violent fate that befalls the speaker if he abandons the right path). سرم in context is 'my head' (the victim of that twisting) — the poetic image of punishment or execution. The clause expresses the consequence: abandoning righteousness would lead, like earlier kings, to my head being turned/twisted (i.e. my downfall).
segments.s325-g9: سیاووش را is a fronted definite object with the object marker را attached to the proper name; پروراننده is a deverbal noun 'nurturer' functioning as the predicate noun, and اوست is the copula (او + ست = 'he is') that completes the nominal predicate. Note the poetic/older spelling ست for است.
segments.s325-g10: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the rest for emphasis; نیکویها is the plural noun 'good things/virtues' and رساننده is another deverbal noun 'bringer/one who delivers', followed by the copula اوست 'he is'. The clause therefore states role/quality: 'he is the bringer of good things to him.'
segments.s325-g11: سپاهی is the noun 'the army/host' and is followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('who/that'); دیدند is past plural 'they saw', and گوپال او is the object 'Gopāl of his' (the name + pronoun), with the pronoun او placed after the name in classical/poetic order.
segments.s325-g12: This is a nominal descriptive list modifying او (the same referent): سر is the head, followed by coordinated nouns ترگ و برز و فر و یال joined with و. There is no finite verb here; the phrase supplies the visual details 'his head — rags, exaltation, glory and mane.' The final او ties the list back to the person seen.
segments.s325-g13: بسی is a quantifier ('much/many') preceding the noun جنگ; جنگ + ناکرده forms a participial phrase 'having not fought (a) battle' where ناکرده is the negative past participle, and the finite verb بگریختند (past plural) 'they fled' is the main verb. The construction = 'many, not having fought, fled.'
segments.s325-g14: همه 'all/everyone' begins the clause; دشت here indicates the location ('the plain') and is placed without a preposition (a common poetic omission), followed by the coordinated objects تیر و کمان ('arrows and bows'). ریختند is the past plural of ریختن 'to pour/throw/spread' used metaphorically: 'they (or it) made the plain be strewn with arrows and bows' (i.e., left them scattered).
segments.s325-g15: به پیش is the prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before', نیاکان من = 'my ancestors' (noun + possessive pronoun), and کینه‌خواه is a predicate noun 'avenger' or 'seeker of vengeance' describing the role — together: 'an avenger before my ancestors.'
segments.s325-g16: چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as'; دستور here functions as 'commander/command' in an older/poetic sense, فرخ is an adjective 'fortunate/happy', and نماینده راه is a compound noun 'one who shows the way' (نماینده = 'one who shows/represents', راه = 'the way'). The whole simile: 'like a fortunate commander who shows the way.'

sentences.s325-s17: Now I have found whatever I sought from desire; what came must (then) go on with a proud stride.
sentences.s325-s18: At daybreak my eyes were heavy from last night; from God came a blessed Soroush (divine messenger).
sentences.s325-s19: which announced that the hour of your departing had come — the end of disgrace and of your sleeplessness.
sentences.s325-s20: Now grief has come upon my court concerning the army, the crown, the throne, and the girdle (the strength/seat of power).
sentences.s325-s1: When Khosrow heard the words from the tales, he cast forth a single learned answer.
sentences.s325-s2: He said to him: “O pure-minded elder, all your counsels and speeches are excellent.”
sentences.s325-s3: From the time of Manuchehr until this time there has been nothing except harmlessness and good intent.
sentences.s325-s4: That famed Rostam of Pīltan — the pillar of the Kians, the pride of the assembly.
sentences.s325-s9: And if I keep your name and suffering in memory, this new tale will remain for a hundred generations.
sentences.s325-s10: If I examine your flattering speech, I will both praise and blame you.
sentences.s325-s11: As for whatever else you asked about my affairs, it is because I was not given burden and harm.
sentences.s325-s12: I had one wish to God: I treated the whole world as contemptible.
sentences.s325-s13: Now it has been five or seven (periods) that I, at his feet, have continually sought guidance from the Judge.
sentences.s325-s14: that he may forgive my past sin and make my dark time bright.
sentences.s325-s15: He took me from this sponge-like dwelling; he was, in every goodness, a guide.
sentences.s325-s16: I cannot abandon this uprightness; if I did, like the kings of old my head would be twisted.
sentences.s325-s5: He is the nurturer of Siyâvash; he is the bringer of good things to him.
sentences.s325-s6: The host, when they saw his Gopāl, beheld his head — rags, exaltation, splendour and mane.
sentences.s325-s7: Many, without having fought a battle, fled; the whole plain was left strewn with arrows and bows.
sentences.s325-s8: An avenger before my ancestors, like a fortunate commander who shows the way.
